title: Why Human Intelligence Matters More in an AI World
gist: RAND's Tom Mulligan argues the conventional wisdom that AI will make human spies obsolete is wrong: AI lowers barriers to technical intelligence collection, which raises the marginal value of human sources (HUMINT) that AI cannot replicate—including access to air-gapped networks, validation of synthetic media, and secure person-to-person communication in a deepfake-saturated world.

themes.theme: AI Paradox: Greater Technical Collection Raises Marginal Value of HUMINT

insights.claim: RAND argues AI will paradoxically increase the importance of human intelligence (HUMINT): as AI lowers barriers to technical collection (open-source encryption, commercial satellite imagery, AI analysis), the marginal value of what AI cannot replicate—human sources inside air-gapped networks or in positions of direct access—rises.
insights.speaker: Tom Mulligan III
insights.so_what: Intelligence budgets and private-sector threat intelligence investments that shift entirely toward AI/technical capabilities are misallocating resources: the RAND argument implies human intelligence programs retain unique, rising value that complements rather than competes with AI collection.
insights.quote: AI will lower many barriers to technical intelligence collection and analysis...Human intelligence is scarcer and harder to replicate, and so its marginal value will rise.
insights.locator: Economics tells us that the value of something

insights.claim: As AI floods electronic channels with fabricated phone calls, forged documents, and synthetic media, human agents become more valuable as validators—helping distinguish authentic source material from forgeries in a disinformation-saturated environment.
insights.speaker: Tom Mulligan III
insights.so_what: The AI-driven disinformation environment creates a specific, growing demand for human verification services—from intelligence agencies but also in financial due diligence, legal discovery, and corporate intelligence contexts where the authenticity of digital evidence is increasingly contested.
insights.quote: Electronic channels will be flooded with fabricated phone calls, forged documents, and other convincing synthetic media. Signals intercepts will be harder to trust on their own. A human agent can help cut through that deluge of disinformation
insights.locator: Human intelligence has always helped validate

insights.claim: As deepfakes make digital communications less trustworthy, the rational response is to distrust electronic communications—making secure person-to-person contact (dead drops, brush passes, brief meetings) the most reliable communication method in an AI-saturated world.
insights.speaker: Tom Mulligan III
insights.so_what: The rise of AI deepfakes argues for investment in secure, verifiable identity infrastructure for high-stakes communications; in corporate espionage and intelligence contexts, traditional offline tradecraft retains strategic value that pure digital security cannot replicate.
insights.quote: Secure, person-to-person communication will rise in value. Case officers have long relied on dead drops, brush passes, and brief meetings to communicate with their agents. In an AI-saturated world, these traditional techniques may be the most reliable methods of communication we have.
insights.locator: AI is making digital communications less trustworthy
